Service of civilians who died overseas or as a result of injury or disease contracted while serving in a civilian capacity with the United States Armed Forces during the dates and/or in areas prescribed is in like manner recognized.

An ornament composed of two ogee curves meeting in the middle, each concave toward its outer extremity and convex toward the point at which it meets the other. Such accolades are either plain or adorned with rich moldings, and are a frequent motive of decoration on the lintels of doors and windows of the fifteenth and sixteenth centuries, especially in secular architecture.
To embrace or kiss in salutation.

Shooting or springing out; projecting.
Denoting any angle less than two right angles.

example: It was a salient choice for the situation.

definitions: An approval, by an authority, generally one that makes something valid.
A penalty, punishment, or some coercive measure, intended to ensure compliance; especially one adopted by several nations, or by an international body.
A law, treaty, or contract, or a clause within a law, treaty, or contract, specifying any of the above.
To ratify; to make valid.
To give official authorization or approval to; to countenance.
To penalize (a State etc.) with sanctions.

example: The article highlighted a sanction in a clear and memorable way.

definitions: To examine something with great care or detail, as to look for hidden or obscure flaws.
To audit accounts etc in order to verify them.

example: Good leaders know when to scrutinize rather than react impulsively.

definitions: Exactly and carefully conducted.
Having scruples or compunctions.
Precise; exact or strict

example: It was a scrupulous choice for the situation.

definitions: Serenity; clearness; calmness.
Evening air; night chill.
To make serene.
Peaceful, calm, unruffled.
Without worry or anxiety; unaffected by disturbance.
Fair and unclouded (as of the sky); clear; unobscured.
Used as part of certain titles, originally to indicate sovereignty or independence.

example: She remained serene even when the discussion became tense.

definitions: A combination of events which have come together by chance to make a surprisingly good or wonderful outcome.
An unsought, unintended, and/or unexpected, but fortunate, discovery and/or learning experience that happens by accident.

example: The report revealed a serendipity that the team had not anticipated.

definitions: Having, or expressing doubt; questioning.
Of or relating to philosophical skepticism or the skeptics.

example: It was a skeptical choice for the situation.

definitions: Disposed to solicit; eager to obtain something desirable, or to avoid anything evil.
(Usually followed by about, for, etc., or a clause) Showing care, concern, or attention, in any of several ways; thus:

example: It was a solicitous choice for the situation.

definitions: Something inducing sleep, especially a drug.
Something boring or dull.
(pharmaceutical effect) Tending to induce sleep.
Boring, dull.

example: It was a soporific choice for the situation.

definitions: Seemingly well-reasoned, plausible or true, but actually fallacious.
Employing fallacious but deceptively plausible arguments; deceitful.
Having an attractive appearance intended to generate a favorable response; deceptively attractive.
Beautiful, pleasing to look at.

example: The argument seemed convincing at first, but on reflection it was entirely specious.

definitions: Someone who spends money improvidently or wastefully.
Improvident, profligate, or wasteful.
Extravagant or lavish.

example: The spendthrift heir quickly depleted the family fortune on frivolous purchases.

definitions: False, not authentic, not genuine.
Extraneous; stray; not relevant or wanted.
Bastardly, illegitimate

example: The report was filled with spurious claims that collapsed under scrutiny.

definitions: (of a voice) Loud, powerful, booming, suitable for giving speeches to large crowds.
(by extension) Stern, authoritarian; demanding of respect.

example: His stentorian voice commanded attention in any room without effort.

definitions: Proponent of stoicism, a school of thought, from in 300 B.C.E. up to about the time of Marcus Aurelius, who holds that by cultivating an understanding of the logos, or natural law, one can be free of suffering.
A person indifferent to pleasure or pain.
Of or relating to the Stoics or their ideas.
Not affected by pain or distress.
Not displaying any external signs of being affected by pain or distress.

example: She remained stoic throughout the long and difficult ordeal.

definitions: One of a class of s-like fricatives produced by an airstream directed at the upper teeth.
Loud; shrill, piercing, high-pitched; rough-sounding
Grating or obnoxious
Vigorous; making strides

example: His strident demands alienated even his closest allies.

definitions: To sublimate.
To raise on high.
To exalt; to heighten; to improve; to purify.
To dignify; to ennoble.

example: The view from the summit at dawn was nothing short of sublime.

definitions: Brief and to the point
Compressed into a tiny area.
Wrapped by, or as if by a girdle; closely fitting, wound or wrapped or drawn up tightly.

example: Her succinct explanation resolved the confusion in under a minute.

definitions: One who uses obsequious compliments to gain self-serving favor or advantage from another; a servile flatterer.
One who seeks to gain through the powerful and influential.
An informer; a talebearer.
To inform against; hence, to calumniate.
To play the sycophant toward; to flatter obsequiously.

example: The manager surrounded himself with sycophants who never challenged his decisions.

definitions: Symbiotic star
Of, or relating to symbiosis; living together.
Of a relationship with mutual benefit between two individuals or organisms.

example: The two companies formed a symbiotic relationship that strengthened both brands.

definitions: (systems theory) A synonym of binding energy.
The cooperation of two or more nerves, muscles, organs, etc.
The combined action of two or more drugs where the effects are stronger than their mere sum.
Benefits resulting from combining different groups, people, objects or processes.

example: The merger created genuine synergy between the two product lines.

definitions: Expressed in silence; implied, but not made explicit; silent.
Not derived from formal principles of reasoning; based on induction rather than deduction.

example: There was a tacit understanding between them that certain topics were off-limits.

definitions: Silent; temperamentally untalkative; disinclined to speak.

example: The taciturn engineer rarely spoke at meetings but always listened carefully.

definitions: Referring to a tangent, moving at a tangent to something.
Merely touching, positioned as a tangent.
Only indirectly related.

example: The discussion became too tangential to be productive.

definitions: Clinging to an object or surface; adhesive.
Unwilling to yield or give up; dogged.
Holding together; cohesive.
Having a good memory; retentive.

example: Her tenacious pursuit of the truth eventually uncovered the scandal.

definitions: (by extension) Of speech or style: brief, concise, to the point.
(by extension) Of manner or speech: abruptly or brusquely short; curt.
Burnished, polished; fine, smooth; neat, spruce.

example: His terse reply made clear he had no interest in further discussion.

definitions: A long, angry or violent speech; a diatribe.
A section of verse concerning a single theme; a laisse.
To make a long, angry or violent speech, a tirade.

example: The coach launched into a tirade after the team gave away a late lead.

definitions: (Oxford University slang) An inferior racing boat, or one who rows in such a boat.
Unmoving
Dormant or hibernating
Lazy, lethargic or apathetic

example: The oppressive heat left everyone torpid and disinclined to work.

definitions: (of people) Capable of being easily led, taught, or managed.
(of a problem) Easy to deal with or manage
Capable of being shaped; malleable.
Capable of being handled or touched.
Sufficiently operationalizable or useful to allow a mathematical calculation to proceed toward a solution.
Of a decision problem, algorithmically solvable fast enough to be practically relevant, typically in polynomial time.

example: The new system proved far more tractable than the one it replaced.

definitions: Something which is transient.
A transient phenomenon, especially an electric current; a very brief surge.
(acoustics) A relatively loud, non-repeating signal in an audio waveform which occurs very quickly, such as the attack of a snare drum.
A person who passes through a place for a short time; a traveller; a migrant worker
A homeless person.
Passing or disappearing with time; transitory.
Remaining for only a brief time.
Decaying with time, especially exponentially.
(stochastic processes, of a state) having a positive probability of being left and never being visited again.
Occasional; isolated; one-off
Passing through; passing from one person to another.
Intermediate.
Operating beyond itself; having an external effect.

example: The discomfort was transient, fading within minutes of the procedure.

definitions: A fearful state; a state of concern or hesitation.
An involuntary trembling, sometimes an effect of paralysis, but usually caused by terror or fear; quaking; quivering.
A libration of the starry sphere in the Ptolemaic system; a motion ascribed to the firmament, to account for certain small changes in the position of the ecliptic and of the stars.

example: She approached the interview with a mixture of excitement and trepidation.

definitions: Often in reference to a word or phrase: used so many times that it is commonplace, or no longer interesting or effective; worn out, hackneyed.
So well established as to be beyond debate: trite law.

example: The speech was full of trite phrases that failed to inspire anyone.

definitions: Cruel or savage.
Deadly or destructive.
Defiant or uncompromising.
Eager or quick to argue, fight or start a conflict.

example: The truculent witness refused to cooperate with either side.

definitions: Violently disturbed or agitated; tempestuous, tumultuous
Being in, or causing, disturbance or unrest

example: It was among the most turbulent periods in the company's history.

definitions: A feeling of anger or annoyance caused by something offensive.
A feeling of doubt.
Leaves that provide shade, as the foliage of trees.
Shadow; shade.
To displease or cause offense.
To shade.

example: He took umbrage at the suggestion that his work needed improvement.

definitions: Something, such as a belief, that cannot be assailed.
Secure against attack; impregnable.
(by extension) Undeniable, incontestable or incontrovertible.

example: She built an unassailable case that left the opposing counsel speechless.

definitions: Unambiguous; without equivocation or ambiguity; singularly clear, unmistakable, or unquestionable
(possibly obsolete) Without equal, matchless.

example: The forensic evidence against him was unequivocal.

definitions: Not able to be held, as of an opinion or position; unholdable, indefensible.
Unfit for habitation

example: Her position became untenable once the full facts came to light.

definitions: (of a man) Courteous, polite, refined, and suave.

example: He was an urbane and cultured host who put every guest at ease.

definitions: Empty; void; lacking meaningful content.
Showing a lack of thought or intelligence; vacant

example: The conversation was pleasant but entirely vacuous.

definitions: Offering nothing that is stimulating or challenging.
Lifeless, dull, or banal.
Tasteless, bland, or insipid.

example: The vapid small talk at the reception left him drained.

definitions: Showing strong feelings; passionate; forceful or intense.

example: She was vehement in her opposition to the proposed changes.

definitions: To treat with great respect and deference.
To revere or hold in awe.

example: The community deeply venerated the founder's legacy.

definitions: Abounding in words, containing more words than necessary; long-winded.
Producing unusually detailed output for diagnostic purposes.

example: His verbose writing style frustrated editors who valued brevity.

definitions: Green in colour.
Abundant in verdure; lush with vegetation.
Fresh.
Inexperienced.

example: The verdant hills stretched as far as the eye could see.

definitions: True; genuine.

example: The discovery was a veritable breakthrough for the entire field.

definitions: Delegated.
Experienced or gained by taking in another person’s experience, rather than through first-hand experience, such as through watching or reading.
On behalf of others.

example: She lived vicariously through her friend's travel stories.

definitions: Watchful, especially for danger or disorder; alert; wary

example: Security staff remained vigilant throughout the duration of the event.

definitions: To clear of an accusation, suspicion or criticism.
To justify by providing evidence.
To maintain or defend (a cause) against opposition.
To provide justification for.
To lay claim to; to assert a right to; to claim.
To liberate; to set free; to deliver.
To avenge; to punish

example: The new evidence fully vindicated the accused and led to the charges being dropped.

definitions: Of or pertaining to vitriol; derived from or resembling vitriol.
Bitterly scathing, caustic.

example: The review was so vitriolic it bordered on a personal attack.

definitions: Making or characterized by a noisy outcry; clamorous.

example: The vociferous crowd made it nearly impossible to hear the speaker.

definitions: A pampered or coddled person.
An overly playful person; a trifler.
A self-indulgent person, fond of excess.
A lewd or immoral person, especially a prostitute.
To rove and ramble without restraint, rule, or limit; to revel; to play loosely; to frolic.
To waste or squander, especially in pleasure (most often with away).
To act wantonly; to be lewd or lascivious.
Undisciplined, unruly; not able to be controlled.
Playful, sportive; merry or carefree.
Lewd, immoral; sexually open, unchaste.
Capricious, reckless of morality, justice etc.; acting without regard for the law or the well-being of others; gratuitous.
Extravagant, unrestrained, excessive.

example: The wanton destruction of the historic building shocked the entire community.

definitions: Cautious of danger; carefully watching and guarding against deception, trickery, and dangers; suspiciously prudent
Characterized by caution; guarded; careful; on one's guard
Thrifty, provident

example: Investors remained wary despite the positive earnings report.

definitions: Charming, engaging, winning; inspiring approval and trust, especially if in an innocent manner.

example: Her winsome manner made her an exceptionally effective negotiator.

definitions: Full of longing or yearning.
Sad and thoughtful.

example: She gave a wistful smile as she leafed through the old photographs.

definitions: To wither; to become, or make, lean and wrinkled by shrinkage, as from age or illness.
Withered; lean and wrinkled by shrinkage as from age or illness.

example: The wizened craftsman still worked with remarkable speed and precision.

definitions: An official providing honorable service in a royal or high noble household, ranking between a squire and a page. Especially, a Yeoman of the Guard, a member of a ceremonial bodyguard to the UK monarch (not to be confused with a Yeoman Warder).
A dependable, diligent, or loyal worker or someone who does a great service.
A former class of small freeholders who farm their own land; a commoner of good standing.
A subordinate, deputy, aide, or assistant.
A Yeoman Warder.
A clerk in the US Navy, and US Coast Guard.
In a vessel of war, the person in charge of the storeroom.
A member of the Yeomanry Cavalry, officially chartered in 1794 originating around the 1760s.
A member of the Imperial Yeomanry, officially created in 1890s and renamed in 1907.
Any of various nymphalid butterflies of the genus Cirrochroa, of Asia and Australasia.

example: He did yeoman work behind the scenes to make the event a success.

definitions: Full of zeal; ardent, fervent; exhibiting enthusiasm or strong passion.

example: The zealous reformer pushed tirelessly for change despite repeated setbacks.

definitions: The point in the sky vertically above a given position or observer; the point in the celestial sphere opposite the nadir.
The highest point in the sky reached by a celestial body.
(by extension) Highest point or state; peak.

example: At the zenith of her career she had few rivals in the field.

definitions: A light wind from the west.
Any light refreshing wind; a gentle breeze.
Anything of fine, soft, or light quality, especially fabric.
To blow or move like a zephyr, or light breeze.
To blow or blow on gently like a zephyr; to cool or refresh with a gentle breeze.

example: A cool zephyr drifted through the open window and eased the afternoon heat.
